---
title: "Chapter 03. Implementing Mean Reversion Strategies"
subtitle: "Overview"
---

# Objective

This chapter turns mean-reversion theory into concrete trading mechanics:

1. choose between price spreads, log price spreads, and ratios as signal spaces
2. replace unconstrained linear scaling with implementable Bollinger-style rules
3. understand why scaling-in is intuitive but not always in-sample optimal
4. use the Kalman filter to estimate time-varying hedge ratios and means
5. reinterpret the Kalman filter as a market-making estimator of fair value
6. recognize how data errors can systematically fake mean-reversion profits

# Sections

## 3.1 Trading Pairs Using Price Spreads, Log Price Spreads, or Ratios

[Open web notes](sections/01-trading-pairs-using-price-spreads-log-price-spreads-or-ratios/web-notes.qmd)

The first section compares three signal constructions for pairs trading and
explains when fixed-share spreads, fixed-capital log spreads, or simple ratios
are the more coherent choice.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/01-trading-pairs-using-price-spreads-log-price-spreads-or-ratios/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/01-trading-pairs-using-price-spreads-log-price-spreads-or-ratios/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 3.2 Bollinger Bands

[Open web notes](sections/02-bollinger-bands/web-notes.qmd)

The second section converts the unconstrained linear strategy into a practical
Bollinger-band rule with explicit entry and exit thresholds and bounded capital
usage.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/02-bollinger-bands/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/02-bollinger-bands/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 3.3 Does Scaling-in Work?

[Open web notes](sections/03-does-scaling-in-work/web-notes.qmd)

The third section analyzes averaging-in mathematically, shows why it is not
in-sample optimal under a simple fixed-probability setup, and explains why it
can still help out of sample.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/03-does-scaling-in-work/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/03-does-scaling-in-work/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 3.4 Kalman Filter as Dynamic Linear Regression

[Open web notes](sections/04-kalman-filter-as-dynamic-linear-regression/web-notes.qmd)

The fourth section introduces the Kalman filter as a disciplined way to update
time-varying hedge ratios, means, and forecast-error volatility without an
arbitrary rolling cutoff.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/04-kalman-filter-as-dynamic-linear-regression/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/04-kalman-filter-as-dynamic-linear-regression/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 3.5 Kalman Filter as Market-Making Model

[Open web notes](sections/05-kalman-filter-as-market-making-model/web-notes.qmd)

The fifth section specializes the Kalman filter to one mean-reverting price
series and reframes it as a fair-value estimator for market making.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/05-kalman-filter-as-market-making-model/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/05-kalman-filter-as-market-making-model/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 3.6 The Danger of Data Errors

[Open web notes](sections/06-the-danger-of-data-errors/web-notes.qmd)

The sixth section explains why bad ticks and quote errors are especially toxic
for mean-reversion backtests and live spread trading.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/06-the-danger-of-data-errors/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/06-the-danger-of-data-errors/pdf-notes.qmd)

# Output Links

Each section owns its own video slides, web notes, and PDF notes. Use the
section folders as the source of truth; shared chapter pages are only indexes.
